commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: No automated repair is needed; the remaining blockers are contributor real behavior proof and maintainer acceptance of the SIGTERM availability fallback.; Cleared: The diff only changes local gateway signal-handler error handling and tests; it does not touch dependencies, workflows, secrets, permissions, networking, or package resolution. (sha=372c906c88ee6d8abe1ed7c368e1ac9f9b45092e)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a catch handler to the gateway SIGTERM async listener that logs failures and requests a stop fallback, plus run-loop regression tests for SIGTERM and existing SIGUSR1 handler errors.

Reproducibility: yes. for source-level reproduction: current main still runs the SIGTERM async handler without a catch, so a thrown lifecycle-runtime load or restart-intent read skips the request path. I did not execute tests in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: No automated repair is needed; the remaining blockers are contributor real behavior proof and maintainer acceptance of the SIGTERM availability fallback.

target.title: fix(gateway): add .catch() to SIGTERM/SIGUSR1 signal handlers
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes the gateway run-loop SIGTERM async handler so lifecycle-runtime failures are caught, logged, and fall back to the normal stop path.
- Keeps the existing main-branch SIGUSR1 recovery behavior: SIGUSR1 handler failures are logged and the already-eager lifecycle runtime is used to clear the restart token.
- Rebased the PR onto current `origin/main`; the original conflicting SIGUSR1 fail-closed implementation was replaced by main's cleaner eager-runtime recovery path.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83116

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: SIGTERM used a discarded async IIFE in the gateway run-loop. If lifecycle-runtime loading or restart-intent consumption rejected inside that handler, the gateway never called the stop/restart request path. This patch catches that rejection, logs it, and falls back to `request("stop", "SIGTERM")`.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node 24 toolchain, current branch `fix/signal-handler-catch` rebased onto `origin/main` at `372c906c88`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`; `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`.

Evidence after fix: The focused run-loop test suite passed with 31 tests. The new regression tests cover SIGTERM handler rejection falling back to stop, SIGUSR1 handler rejection clearing the restart token through the eager lifecycle runtime, and the SIGUSR1 cleanup path staying non-fatal if token cleanup itself throws. Autoreview returned `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

Observed result after fix: A simulated SIGTERM lifecycle-runtime error no longer leaves the gateway half-alive. The handler logs `failed to handle SIGTERM: ...`, calls the normal stop request path, closes the server with `reason: "gateway stopping"`, and exits with code 0 in the harness.

What was not tested: A live production gateway with a physically missing runtime chunk after package replacement was not rerun after the rebase. The current branch no longer uses the original SIGUSR1 fail-closed path; SIGUSR1 now relies on main's eager lifecycle runtime recovery, covered by the run-loop tests.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: If SIGTERM restart-intent consumption fails, the fallback stops instead of restarting.
- Mitigation: This is safer than hanging; the failure is logged and the operator can restart normally.
target.author: sebtardif
